content.text: Producer-Consumer SLA Terms in Data Contracts

A data contract becomes enforceable only when both sides understand exactly what the producer promises and what the consumer can depend on. SLA terms are the binding clauses that specify these commitments quantitatively: how fresh the data must be, how complete each batch must arrive, how quickly latency breaches must be escalated, and what happens when a term is violated. In GenAI pipelines these terms directly determine retrieval quality — stale embeddings produce hallucinations, missing chunks break citation coverage, and classification errors leak confidential content to unauthorised consumers.

attrs.alt: Producer-Consumer SLA lifecycle in data contracts

content.text: Data contract SLAs split into two families:
that constrain the content of each delivered batch, and
that constrain the timing and responsiveness of the producer.
— the fraction of records where required fields are non-null and non-empty. A threshold of 0.99 means at most 1 in 100 records may be defective. For GenAI this guards against chunks that lack
timestamps in the batch. A contract might state that 95% of records must be less than 24 hours old, measured from the source system's commit time to embedding write time.

— the fraction of (record, field) pairs that are null across a defined set of required fields. Tracks silent data loss that completeness alone does not catch (completeness checks full-record emptiness; null rate catches partial missing fields).
— the fraction of records that share a (
) key with another record in the batch. The target is always 0.0 for deterministic lookup — any duplicate produces retrieval inconsistency.
matches the allowed-values enum. Target is 1.0; any unlabelled record must be rejected at ingest rather than silently routed.

— wall-clock time from source commit to the record being queryable in the vector index. Measured end-to-end, not just pipeline throughput. A 4-hour p95 target is typical for HR policy data updated once per day.

— the maximum elapsed time between a monitor detecting a violation and the consumer receiving an alert. 15 minutes is a standard enterprise target; it lets RAG pipelines apply fallback strategies before users encounter degraded answers.

— the advance notice period (in business days) for scheduled maintenance that will interrupt data delivery. Gives consumer teams time to enable cached fallbacks or suppress AI features during the window.
— P1 (critical breach, first response ≤ 1 hour) and P2 (moderate breach, ≤ 4 hours). Severity is determined by the ratio of actual to threshold — greater than 5× the threshold is always P1.

The YAML block below shows a complete set of SLA terms for an HR policy data contract. Both quality SLAs and operational SLAs are expressed declaratively so monitoring tools can load and enforce them without manual configuration.

# contract: hr-policy-docs → rag-platform  v1.2.0
id: hr-policy-docs-rag-v1
version: "1.2.0"
status: active
effective_date: "2024-03-01"

producer:
  team: data-platform
  contact: data-platform@example.com
  on_call_channel: "#data-platform-incidents"

consumers:
  - team: rag-platform
    contact: rag-ops@example.com
    permitted_apps: [internal-copilot, hr-chatbot]

operational_slas:
  ingestion_latency:
    description: "Time from source update to index ready"
    target_p95_hours: 4
    measurement: "wall-clock from source_updated_at to embedding timestamp"

  breach_notification:
    description: "Max time from detected breach to consumer alert"
    target_minutes: 15
    channel: slack:#rag-sla-alerts

  planned_downtime_notice:
    description: "Advance notice for scheduled maintenance windows"
    target_business_days: 5
    method: email + calendar invite

  incident_response:
    P1:  # >25% data missing or >24h stale
      first_response_minutes: 60
      resolution_hours: 4
    P2:  # 5-25% missing or 8-24h stale
      first_response_minutes: 240
      resolution_hours: 24

quality_slas:
  completeness:
    field: content
    min: 0.99
    measurement: non-null non-empty ratio per batch

  freshness:
    field: source_updated_at
    max_age_hours: 24
    percentile: 95

  null_rate:
    fields: [doc_id, chunk_id, content, classification_label]
    max: 0.01

  duplicate_rate:
    key: [doc_id, chunk_id]
    max: 0.0

  classification_validity:
    field: classification_label
    allowed_values: [public, internal, confidential, restricted]
    min_validity: 1.0

schema_stability:
  notice_period_days: 30   # for MAJOR version bumps
  deprecation_grace_days: 14
  backward_compatible_changes: MINOR   # new optional fields, relaxed SLAs
  breaking_changes: MAJOR              # field removal, type change, embedding_model change

Design note: express SLA thresholds as the minimum acceptable quality, not the average observed quality. If current completeness averages 99.8%, set the SLA at 99.0% to provide a breach buffer for anomalous batches. Setting SLAs equal to the current average means any fluctuation immediately triggers breach alerts, causing alert fatigue.
The classes below load a contract's SLA terms and evaluate each arriving batch. A
is raised for every term that fails, with a computed severity that drives the notification tier.

import statistics
from datetime import datetime, timezone

class SLAMonitor:
    """Evaluates a data batch against the SLA terms in a contract."""

    def __init__(self, contract: dict):
        self.contract_id = contract["id"]
        self.slas = contract["quality_slas"]
        self.op_slas = contract["operational_slas"]

    def check(self, batch: list[dict], ingestion_latency_hours: float) -> SLAMonitorResult:
        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
        result = SLAMonitorResult(contract_id=self.contract_id, checked_at=now)
        n = len(batch)
        if n == 0:
            return result

        # --- completeness ---
        complete = sum(1 for r in batch if r.get("content") not in (None, ""))
        completeness = complete / n
        threshold = self.slas["completeness"]["min"]
        if completeness < threshold:
            term = SLATerm(SLATermType.COMPLETENESS, threshold, "ratio")
            result.violations.append(SLAViolation(term, completeness, now, self.contract_id))

        # --- null rate (across required fields) ---
        required = ["doc_id", "chunk_id", "content", "classification_label"]
        null_count = sum(1 for r in batch for f in required if r.get(f) is None)
        null_rate = null_count / (n * len(required))
        null_threshold = self.slas["null_rate"]["max"]
        if null_rate > null_threshold:
            term = SLATerm(SLATermType.NULL_RATE, null_threshold, "ratio")
            result.violations.append(SLAViolation(term, null_rate, now, self.contract_id))

        # --- duplicate rate ---
        seen = set()
        dupes = 0
        for r in batch:
            key = (r.get("doc_id"), r.get("chunk_id"))
            if key in seen:
                dupes += 1
            seen.add(key)
        dup_rate = dupes / n
        dup_threshold = self.slas["duplicate_rate"]["max"]
        if dup_rate > dup_threshold:
            term = SLATerm(SLATermType.DUPLICATE_RATE, dup_threshold, "ratio")
            result.violations.append(SLAViolation(term, dup_rate, now, self.contract_id))

        # --- classification validity ---
        allowed = set(self.slas["classification_validity"]["allowed_values"])
        valid_count = sum(1 for r in batch if r.get("classification_label") in allowed)
        validity = valid_count / n
        if validity < self.slas["classification_validity"]["min_validity"]:
            term = SLATerm(SLATermType.COMPLETENESS, 1.0, "ratio")  # reuse COMPLETENESS type
            result.violations.append(SLAViolation(term, validity, now, self.contract_id))

        # --- ingestion latency ---
        latency_threshold = self.op_slas["ingestion_latency"]["target_p95_hours"]
        if ingestion_latency_hours > latency_threshold:
            term = SLATerm(SLATermType.INGESTION_LATENCY, latency_threshold, "hours", 95)
            result.violations.append(SLAViolation(term, ingestion_latency_hours, now, self.contract_id))

        return result


def notify_on_breach(result: SLAMonitorResult) -> None:
    """Route violations to the correct channel within breach_notification SLA."""
    if result.healthy:
        return
    p1 = [v for v in result.violations if v.severity == "P1"]
    p2 = [v for v in result.violations if v.severity == "P2"]
    if p1:
        _page_oncall(result.contract_id, p1)
    if p2:
        _slack_alert(result.contract_id, p2)

def _page_oncall(contract_id: str, violations: list[SLAViolation]) -> None:
    # Integrate with PagerDuty / OpsGenie — breach notification ≤15 min
    for v in violations:
        print(f"[P1] {contract_id} | {v.term.term_type.value}: actual={v.actual:.3f} threshold={v.term.threshold}")

def _slack_alert(contract_id: str, violations: list[SLAViolation]) -> None:
    for v in violations:
        print(f"[P2] {contract_id} | {v.term.term_type.value}: actual={v.actual:.3f} threshold={v.term.threshold}")
Freshness requires computing percentile age across the batch rather than a simple ratio, so it gets its own helper:

from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

def check_freshness_sla(
    batch: list[dict],
    max_age_hours: float,
    percentile: int = 95,
) -> tuple[bool, float]:
    """Returns (passes, p95_age_hours)."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    ages = []
    for record in batch:
        ts_str = record.get("source_updated_at")
        if ts_str is None:
            ages.append(float("inf"))  # missing timestamp = maximally stale
            continue
        ts = datetime.fromisoformat(ts_str)
        if ts.tzinfo is None:
            ts = ts.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)
        ages.append((now - ts).total_seconds() / 3600.0)

    ages.sort()
    idx = int(len(ages) * percentile / 100)
    p_age = ages[min(idx, len(ages) - 1)]
    return p_age <= max_age_hours, p_age
The example below runs the monitor against a batch that contains a duplicate, triggering a P2 alert:

# Load and parse contract (in practice, from the contract registry)
import yaml

contract_yaml = """
id: hr-policy-docs-rag-v1
quality_slas:
  completeness: {min: 0.99}
  null_rate: {fields: [doc_id, chunk_id, content, classification_label], max: 0.01}
  duplicate_rate: {key: [doc_id, chunk_id], max: 0.0}
  classification_validity:
    allowed_values: [public, internal, confidential, restricted]
    min_validity: 1.0
operational_slas:
  ingestion_latency: {target_p95_hours: 4}
  breach_notification: {target_minutes: 15}
"""
contract = yaml.safe_load(contract_yaml)

monitor = SLAMonitor(contract)

# Sample batch arriving from the pipeline
batch = [
    {"doc_id": "d1", "chunk_id": "c1", "content": "Policy text...",
     "classification_label": "internal"},
    {"doc_id": "d2", "chunk_id": "c2", "content": "Another chunk",
     "classification_label": "confidential"},
    {"doc_id": "d1", "chunk_id": "c1", "content": "Policy text...",   # duplicate!
     "classification_label": "internal"},
]

result = monitor.check(batch, ingestion_latency_hours=3.2)
if not result.healthy:
    notify_on_breach(result)
    # → [P2] hr-policy-docs-rag-v1 | duplicate_rate: actual=0.333 threshold=0.0
SLA terms are not set unilaterally by either side. The process is collaborative and documented:
: the producer team documents current p95 latency, actual completeness rates, and observed null rates from their monitoring dashboards.
: the consumer team identifies which SLA dimensions are blocking for their use case. A real-time chatbot has tighter freshness requirements than a quarterly-report summariser.

: where consumer requirements exceed producer capabilities, the teams agree either to invest in infrastructure improvements or to accept degraded behaviour (e.g., the consumer adds a freshness gate that suppresses answers for stale content).
: the agreed terms are captured in a short Architecture Decision Record and attached to the contract PR for audit.
: a minimum of 5 business days review for new contracts or MAJOR SLA changes, giving legal and compliance teams time to sign off.
SLA terms follow the same semver rules as the schema:
Producer improves guarantee; consumers gain, never lose
Consumer pipelines may be unable to cope with weaker guarantees
Adds coverage without removing existing guarantees
Consumers may depend on the guarantee being monitored
Operational detail; no consumer logic changes

Any relaxation of an SLA is a breaking change for consumers even if the schema is unchanged. A RAG pipeline that caches data for 20 hours depends on the freshness SLA being ≤ 24 hours; relaxing it to 48 hours silently invalidates that caching strategy.
SLA terms impose obligations on consumers, not just producers. Well-formed contracts include:
: consumers must implement fallback behaviour (cached answers, topic deflection, explicit staleness warnings) for periods when quality SLAs are actively breached.
: consumer teams must acknowledge P1 breach alerts within the contracted response window and confirm whether they have activated fallback strategies.
: consumers must subscribe to the contract's test data feed and run integration tests against schema changes before a MAJOR version goes active.
: consumers log retrieval quality signals (answer relevance scores, citation failure rates) and share quarterly aggregates with the producer so SLA targets can be refined empirically.
Beyond per-batch monitoring, track SLA adherence as a time-series metric:
per contract per term per hour (0.0 to 1.0).
Alert when the 7-day rolling average drops below 0.95 (95% of batches passing) to catch chronic partial degradation.
Publish a weekly SLA report to both producer and consumer stakeholders showing trend lines per term.
Escalate to contract renegotiation if any term shows <0.9 compliance over 30 consecutive days.

Producer-consumer SLA terms split into quality SLAs (completeness, freshness, null rate, duplicate rate, classification validity) and operational SLAs (ingestion latency, breach notification time, incident response tiers).
Express all SLA terms declaratively in the YAML contract so monitors can load and enforce them without code changes.
The SLAMonitor class evaluates each arriving batch against every term and raises typed SLAViolation objects with computed severity.
Freshness requires percentile computation across the batch, not a simple pass/fail per record.
Relaxing an SLA is a MAJOR version bump and requires 30 days notice because consumers build operational strategies that assume the current guarantee.
Consumers are parties to the contract, not just recipients: they must implement fallback behaviour, acknowledge breach alerts, and share retrieval quality feedback.
